summary.intent: Build a system that maps checkpoint IDs (extracted from git commit messages) to git commit hashes (OIDs), storing the mapping in a new database table populated during checkpoint indexing.
summary.outcome: Created `src/gitUtils.ts` with helpers to extract checkpoint IDs from commit messages and find commits by checkpoint ID, added the `CheckpointIdGitOidJoin` Prisma table, wired the mapping save into the indexer, and verified 39 rows were populated after fixing an `isNew` guard that prevented backfilling already-indexed checkpoints.

repo: Checkpoints are associated with git commits whose message body contains a line matching `Entire-Checkpoint: <id>`.
The indexer lives in `src/indexer.ts`; `indexCheckpointV2` / `indexAllCheckpointsV2` are the active code paths; the V1 `indexCheckpoint` function was removed during this session.
The repo path is available as `repoDir` at the server call site in `src/server.ts`.
All 39 checkpoints were already indexed before the OID-mapping feature landed, so any backfill logic must run unconditionally rather than only for new checkpoints.
TypeScript narrowing breaks when a boolean alias (`isNew`) is used instead of directly checking the nullable variable — use a non-null assertion after the if/else block.

code.finding: Contains `getCheckpointIdFromCommitMessage` (regex `/^Entire-Checkpoint:\s*(\S+)$/m`) and `findCommitForCheckpoint` which searches the provided branch then falls back to `main`/`master`.
`saveGitOidMapping` helper skips early via `findFirst` if mapping already exists, making repeated index passes cheap. Originally guarded by `isNew`, which was removed to allow backfilling.
`CheckpointIdGitOidJoin` uses `gitOid` as primary key with an index on `checkpointId`; a single checkpoint ID can theoretically map to multiple git commits.

workflow: The `gitlog` npm package requires an absolute repo path and uses a callback API; tested experimentally before writing production code.
When a Prisma schema changes, both `prisma db push` and a TypeScript rebuild are needed to regenerate the client.
Background server processes can die silently — always verify with a health-check request after starting.

friction: Multiple rounds of Bash experimentation were needed to discover the correct `gitlog` API shape (callback vs promise, path requirements, return type).
TypeScript narrowing through a boolean alias (`isNew`) failed silently at build time, requiring a non-null assertion workaround.
The `isNew` guard caused a silent bug where no rows were ever written for already-indexed checkpoints — only caught by querying the table after the server restarted.
Many Read tool calls on `indexer.ts` were needed to navigate the file, suggesting the file is large and context was repeatedly lost.
open_items: The `saveGitOidMapping` call now runs on every index pass for every checkpoint; a more targeted approach (e.g., only for checkpoints missing a mapping) could reduce unnecessary DB reads at scale.
No tests were written for `getCheckpointIdFromCommitMessage` or `findCommitForCheckpoint`.
The fallback branch search order (provided branch → main → master) is hardcoded; could be made configurable.
